content_en:
The substantive range of the Wafayat al-A'yan is remarkable for a medieval biographical dictionary. Alongside entries on major jurists, hadith scholars, theologians, and Qur'anic commentators — the expected content of Islamic biographical literature — Ibn Khallikan includes substantial entries on poets, physicians, philosophers, mathematicians, astronomers, historians, geographers, architects, and musicians. This cultural comprehensiveness reflects a vision of Islamic civilization that encompasses all of its intellectual and artistic achievements.

The entries on Abbasid court culture are particularly vivid. Ibn Khallikan preserves detailed accounts of the literary salons, poetry competitions, and musical performances that characterized the high culture of Baghdad at its peak. His entries on famous poets like Abu Nuwas, al-Mutanabbi, and Abu Tammam contain extensive samples of their verse along with biographical information about their lives and relationships with patrons. These sections make the Wafayat an important source for the history of Arabic literature.

The entries on scholars of the Islamic sciences are thorough and often contain information not available elsewhere. Ibn Khallikan's account of al-Ghazali, for instance, is one of the most detailed medieval accounts of this pivotal figure. His entry on al-Hariri, author of the famous Maqamat, provides important information about this literary genre and its social context. The entries on major hadith scholars provide genealogical and biographical information that complements the specialized rijal literature.

A notable theme is the experience of scholars under different political regimes. Ibn Khallikan documents how scholars' fortunes rose and fell with changes of dynasty, how some achieved great honors and others suffered persecution, and how the relationship between the scholarly establishment and political power shaped individual careers. This attention to the political context of scholarly life gives the Wafayat a dimension of social history that goes beyond mere enumeration of dates and works.

Ibn Khallikan also shows strong interest in the etymology of names and epithets, regularly providing explanations of place names from which scholars' nisbas derive and clarifying the meanings of titles and honorifics. This linguistic dimension reflects his formation in classical Arabic philology alongside jurisprudence.

The treatment of non-Arab scholars is significant. Ibn Khallikan includes Persians, Turks, Berbers, and other ethnic groups who made major contributions to Islamic civilization, demonstrating that by his time the cultural identity of Islamic civilization had become genuinely multi-ethnic rather than merely Arab. His entries on Persian poets who wrote in Arabic, or on scholars of Central Asian origin who became authorities of the Islamic sciences, illustrate this cultural synthesis.
